content:
Looking at this patch, I can identify a potential issue related to the removal of `m_NotForbidUndef` and its replacement with `m_Not`. The key concern is that `m_Not` (via `m_AllOnes()`) now only accepts poison elements in vector splats, not undef elements. This could cause pattern matches to fail in cases where vectors contain undef elements, potentially breaking optimizations that previously worked.

## Analysis of the Issue

The patch changes `cstval_pred_ty` to only ignore poison elements, not undef elements. This affects all pattern matchers like `m_AllOnes()`, `m_Zero()`, etc. The `m_NotForbidUndef` helper was specifically created to handle cases where we need to ensure all elements are -1 (not just non-undef elements being -1). Now that `m_Not` uses `m_AllOnes()` which rejects vectors with undef elements, some optimizations might no longer trigger.

Specifically, in `InstructionSimplify.cpp`, several patterns that used `m_NotForbidUndef` now use `m_Not`. If the input contains undef elements, `m_Not` will fail to match, causing these optimizations to be missed.

## Test Case to Expose the Issue

Here's a test case that demonstrates the problem. The optimization `(B ^ ~A) | (A & B) --> B ^ ~A` should fire, but with undef elements in the vector, it won't match after this patch:

```llvm
; RUN: opt -passes=instsimplify -S %s

define <2 x i8> @test_or_simplify_undef(<2 x i8> %a, <2 x i8> %b) {
  %not_a = xor <2 x i8> %a, <i8 -1, i8 undef>  ; Note: undef in second element
  %xor = xor <2 x i8> %b, %not_a
  %and = and <2 x i8> %a, %b
  %or = or <2 x i8> %xor, %and
  ret <2 x i8> %or
}
```

Before the patch, this would simplify to `%xor` (i.e., `B ^ ~A`) because `m_NotForbidUndef` would reject the pattern due to the undef element. Wait, actually `m_NotForbidUndef` was created to *forbid* undef, so it wouldn't match either. Let me reconsider...

Actually, looking more carefully at the code: `m_NotForbidUndef` used `m_APIntForbidUndef` which would reject vectors with undef elements. So the change from `m_NotForbidUndef` to `m_Not` should actually make the pattern *more* permissive (allowing poison but not undef), not less.

But wait, `m_Not` uses `m_AllOnes()` which now rejects undef elements. So actually, `m_Not` will fail to match vectors with undef elements, while `m_NotForbidUndef` would also fail. So this might not be a regression in terms of matching ability.

However, there's a more subtle issue. Let me look at the actual change in `cstval_pred_ty`:

```cpp
// Before: if (isa<UndefValue>(Elt)) continue;
// After:  if (isa<PoisonValue>(Elt)) continue;
```

This means that vectors with undef elements will no longer match patterns like `m_AllOnes()`, `m_Zero()`, etc. This could break optimizations that previously worked with undef elements.

Here's a better test case showing the actual regression:

```llvm
; RUN: opt -passes=instsimplify -S %s

define <2 x i8> @test_ashr_allones_undef(<2 x i8> %x) {
  ; -1 >>a X --> -1 should still work even with undef elements
  %ashr = ashr exact <2 x i8> <i8 -1, i8 undef>, %x
  ret <2 x i8> %ashr
}
```

The simplification `-1 >>a X --> -1` uses `match(Op0, m_AllOnes())`. With undef in the vector, `m_AllOnes()` will now return false, so the optimization won't trigger. The comment in the code even says "Do not return Op0 because it may contain undef elements if it's a vector" was changed to "We could return the original -1 constant to preserve poison elements."

Actually, looking at the `simplifyAShrInst` change:
```cpp
// Before: if (match(Op0, m_AllOnes()) || ...) return Constant::getAllOnesValue(...);
// After:  if (match(Op0, m_AllOnes()) || ...) return Constant::getAllOnesValue(...);
```

The code is the same, but `m_AllOnes()` now rejects undef elements. So the optimization won't fire for vectors with undef elements.

## Another Test Case

```llvm
; RUN: opt -passes=instcombine -S %s

define <2 x i8> @test_and_shl_zext_undef(<2 x i8> %x, <2 x i8> %y) {
  %zext = zext <2 x i8> %x to <2 x i16>
  %shl = shl <2 x i16> %zext, %y  ; y could have undef elements
  %and = and <2 x i16> %shl, <i16 -32768, i16 undef>  ; sign mask with undef
  ret <2 x i16> %and
}
```

The pattern `and(shl(zext(X), Y), SignMask) -> and(sext(X), SignMask)` in InstCombine uses `m_SignMask()` which relies on `cstval_pred_ty`. With undef in the sign mask, it won't match. The patch removed the undef sanitization code, assuming the pattern won't match with undef elements.

## Conclusion

The patch intentionally removes support for undef elements in constant pattern matchers. This is a design choice to move away from undef toward poison. The potential issue is that optimizations that previously worked with undef elements might no longer trigger. However, given that LLVM has been moving toward poison for years, and tests have been updated to use poison instead of undef, this is likely an acceptable change.

The real risk would be if there are production code patterns that still generate vectors with undef elements (not poison) that need these optimizations. The patch author believes this is no longer the case based on the historical context provided in the commit message.
